name: Thymosin Alpha-1 (Tα1)
summary: Thymosin Alpha-1 is a synthetic 28-amino-acid peptide identical to a naturally occurring fragment of prothymosin alpha produced by the thymus. Marketed as thymalfasin (Zadaxin) in more than 30 countries, it is an immune-modulator studied for chronic hepatitis B and C, sepsis, cancer immunotherapy support, and vaccine response.
mechanism:
Thymosin Alpha-1 restores and modulates immune homeostasis rather than acting as a simple stimulant. It promotes the maturation, differentiation, and proliferation of T lymphocytes in the thymus, tending to raise CD4+ T-cell counts and normalize the CD4+/CD8+ ratio, and it enhances natural killer (NK) cell activity.

At the molecular level Tα1 signals through Toll-like receptors (notably TLR2, TLR3/4, and TLR9), activating downstream IRF3, NF-κB, p38 MAPK, and MyD88 pathways. This drives dendritic-cell maturation and antigen presentation, bridging innate and adaptive immunity and increasing production of Th1 cytokines such as IL-2, IFN-γ, and IL-12 while dampening pro-inflammatory overactivation.

Because each dose triggers sustained dendritic-cell and T-cell activation, the immunological effects persist for roughly 72–96 hours even though the peptide itself is cleared within a day — the rationale for the twice-weekly clinical dosing schedule. In septic patients Tα1 has been shown to restore monocyte HLA-DR expression, a marker of reversed immunoparalysis.
benefits: Immune modulation: increases CD4+ T cells and normalizes the CD4+/CD8+ ratio
Approved (as thymalfasin/Zadaxin) in 30+ countries for chronic hepatitis B and C, often with interferon
Reduced 28-day mortality trend and restored monocyte HLA-DR in the ETASS severe-sepsis trial
Enhances dendritic-cell maturation, NK-cell activity, and antigen presentation
Studied as an adjunct to improve vaccine responses in immunocompromised or elderly patients
Investigated as supportive immunotherapy in cancer and in COVID-19
Generally well tolerated with a favorable safety profile in clinical trials
risks: Not FDA-approved in the United States (approved abroad as thymalfasin/Zadaxin); U.S. use is off-label/research only and it was placed on the FDA 503A/503B bulk restriction review
Human dosing outside the labeled hepatitis indication (sepsis, cancer, longevity) is investigational and not standardized
Should be avoided or used only under specialist supervision in organ-transplant recipients or others on intentional immunosuppression, as immune activation could promote rejection
Theoretical caution in autoimmune disease due to immune stimulation
May be prohibited by some sporting/anti-doping frameworks as an immunomodulator; verify current WADA status before competition
sideEffects: Injection-site reactions: redness, discomfort, or transient erythema
Generally mild and infrequent; large trials reported no serious drug-related adverse events
Occasional transient fatigue, joint discomfort, or low-grade flu-like feeling

dosage.notes: The best-established clinical dose (thymalfasin/Zadaxin for chronic hepatitis B) is 1.6 mg (approximately 900 mcg/m2) subcutaneously twice weekly, injections spaced 3–4 days apart. Some sepsis/critical-care protocols used higher loading (e.g. 1.6 mg twice daily for several days, then once daily). Educational information only, not medical advice.
dosage.timing: Twice weekly with roughly 3–4 days between injections. Because immune effects persist ~72–96 hours per dose, this schedule maintains modulation despite the ~2-hour plasma half-life. Timing relative to food is not critical.
dosage.maxDose: The labeled hepatitis dose is 1.6 mg twice weekly for 6–12 months. Short-term critical-care regimens have used up to ~3.2 mg/day in divided doses; no higher chronic ceiling is established for non-hospital use.

steps.note: 1.6 mg subcutaneously twice weekly (~3–4 days apart) for 6 months; the labeled thymalfasin course for chronic hepatitis B runs 6–12 months. Sustained virological benefit is often delayed until ~12 months post-treatment.
1.6 mg twice weekly for ~4 weeks as a general educational immune-support block; anecdotal and not a validated indication.

frequency: twice weekly (3–4 days apart); some acute protocols dose daily
halfLife: Rapidly absorbed with peak serum levels at ~2 hours and clearance to baseline by ~24 hours; downstream immunological effects persist ~72–96 hours
cycleLength: 6–12 months for the hepatitis indication; shorter 4–12 week educational immune-support blocks are also used

reconstitution.notes: A 10 mg research vial + 2 mL bacteriostatic water = 5 mg/mL, so a 1.6 mg dose = 0.32 mL (32 units on a U-100 insulin syringe). The commercial Zadaxin presentation is a 1.6 mg lyophilized vial reconstituted with 1.0 mL sterile diluent and injected whole. Swirl gently; do not shake.

storage: Store lyophilized vials refrigerated at 2–8°C (frozen for long-term). After reconstitution, refrigerate at 2–8°C and use within ~2–4 weeks; protect from light and do not freeze the reconstituted solution.
contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to thymosin alpha-1 or thymalfasin
Organ-transplant recipients and others on deliberate immunosuppression (risk of promoting rejection) unless supervised by a specialist
Active autoimmune disease (use only under specialist guidance due to immune stimulation)
Competitive athletes should verify current anti-doping status before use

interactions.note: Frequently confused with Thymosin Beta-4 (TB-500) because of the shared 'thymosin' name, but they are unrelated molecules with different actions — Tα1 is an immune modulator, TB-500 is a tissue-repair/actin-binding peptide. Do not substitute one for the other.

Theoretically complementary immune modulation — Tα1 restores adaptive T-cell and dendritic-cell function while KPV calms excess inflammation. Combined use is not clinically validated; educational only.
